title: GPU Diagnostics
use_for: kernel, buffer, and material binding readiness checks
do_not_use_for: core compute algorithm design without diagnostics concern
rules: diagnostics must not become a required hot-path synchronization point
prefer GPU counters with throttled async readback
validate kernels, buffers, and shader bindings before dispatch or draw
track readback in-flight state to avoid request pileups
make optional diagnostics degrade without breaking rendering
report missing required resources clearly and early
procedure: Define which GPU stages need counters or health signals.
Define counter buffer layout and ownership.
Write counters in kernels or stage handoff code.
Read counters asynchronously at a bounded interval.
Expose readiness for kernels, buffers, draw args, and shader bindings.
Classify failures as required pipeline errors or optional diagnostic loss.

primary_when: the request is specifically about GPU counters, readback, pipeline health, missing kernels, buffer warnings, or indirect args validation
the request is about diagnosing a GPU pipeline without stalling the frame
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about compute algorithm design, indirect rendering design, or CPU-only diagnostics

solves: making GPU pipeline state inspectable without blocking the CPU every frame
distinguishing required GPU resource failures from optional diagnostic loss
catching stale buffers, missing kernels, unbound shader buffers, invalid args, and readback misuse early
does_not_solve: full profiling methodology outside GPU pipeline surfaces
performance tuning without measured counters or profiler data

core_principles: GPU diagnostics should explain pipeline health, not define the pipeline's correctness path.
Counters belong close to the stages that produce the facts.
Async readback should be throttled, tracked, and optional.
Required resource failures should be clear before dispatch or draw.
Safe dummy bindings or clean draw skips are better than repeated graphics API warnings.
Diagnostics should preserve stage boundaries and buffer ownership.
constraints: Do not use blocking readback as a routine frame-loop diagnostic.
Do not enqueue unlimited async readback requests.
Do not require debug buffers for release rendering correctness.
Do not ignore missing compute kernels or material buffer bindings.
Do not mutate shared material state unpredictably while diagnosing bindings.
Do not hide indirect args failures behind zero-instance draws without a reason code.
decision_rules: Use gpu-diagnostics as primary when the task is about counters, async readback, missing resources, GPU warnings, or pipeline health.
Attach gpu-pipeline-architecture when diagnostics must be tied to stage state and rerun policy.
Attach gpu-compute when diagnostics are written by kernels or validate dispatch inputs.
Attach gpu-instancing when diagnostics validate draw args, visible counts, shader buffers, or draw bounds.
Use async readback for debug counters and avoid readback for normal draw or dispatch count flow.
Treat missing required kernels and buffers as pipeline errors, but optional debug readback errors as diagnostic degradation.

preferred_patterns: small structured debug counter buffer per pipeline unit
stage-local counter increments for spawned, valid, visible, rejected, or culled records
bounded async readback interval with in-flight tracking
kernel and buffer validation before dispatch
safe dummy buffers for required shader bindings before real data exists
readiness snapshots for stage, buffer, and draw state
avoid_patterns: synchronous GetData in runtime hot paths
diagnostic readback every frame for every chunk
graphics API warnings used as normal control flow
debug counters required for release draw correctness
unbounded log spam for repeated missing resource failures

recipes.name: Add throttled GPU debug counters
recipes.when: Use when a staged GPU pipeline needs visibility into stage throughput or rejection reasons.
steps: Define a compact counter layout for the pipeline unit.
Allocate the counter buffer with the owning chunk, batch, or stage state.
Reset counters only when the measured stage reruns.
Increment counters in the relevant kernels.
Request AsyncGPUReadback at a bounded interval.
Track in-flight requests and drop new requests until the prior one completes.

recipes.name: Validate indirect rendering readiness
recipes.when: Use when DrawMeshInstancedIndirect produces missing draws, warnings, or suspicious zero counts.
steps: Check instance buffer, visible index buffer, input count buffer, draw args buffer, mesh, material, and bounds.
Validate that append counters are reset before visibility kernels.
Validate that CopyCount feeds the same count buffer used by the args kernel.
Check draw args fields against mesh index count, visible count, start index, base vertex, and start instance.
Bind safe dummy buffers or skip draw when required buffers are unavailable.
Record whether the failure is data absence, binding absence, args invalidity, or bounds clipping.

recipes.name: Classify GPU pipeline failures
recipes.when: Use when a GPU feature must degrade cleanly instead of producing silent no-op behavior.
steps: Classify missing compute shader, missing kernel, missing required buffer, invalid dispatch args, invalid draw args, and missing material binding separately.
Fail required stages before dispatch or draw.
Disable optional diagnostics when readback or debug counters fail.
Expose a readiness snapshot or reason code for dependent systems.
Avoid repeated logs after the same failure has been reported.

pitfalls: Using readback as the normal way to know how many instances to draw.
Starting a new AsyncGPUReadback every frame without checking in-flight state.
Treating zero visible instances as success without validating args and bounds.
Letting unbound buffers generate graphics warnings every render pass.
Resetting debug counters at the wrong stage and hiding useful data.
Logging every frame for the same missing kernel or buffer.
review_checklist: Are diagnostics optional unless explicitly required for correctness?
Are readbacks asynchronous, throttled, and in-flight tracked?
Are required kernels and buffers validated before dispatch?
Are shader buffers bound safely before draw or skipped cleanly?
Can indirect args be inspected or reasoned about without CPU hot-path readback?
Are failure categories and degraded states explicit?
Does diagnostics preserve stage and buffer ownership boundaries?

references.title: GPU Diagnostics
references.interpretation: Use this first for counters, async readback, readiness, and failure policy.
references.why: It defines how GPU observability stays non-blocking and explicit.

references.title: GPU Pipeline Architecture
references.interpretation: Use when diagnostics must map onto stage contracts and dirty state.
references.why: Diagnostics should follow the same stage ownership model as the pipeline.

references.title: Indirect Rendering
references.interpretation: Use when diagnosing visible counts, draw args, buffer bindings, or indirect draw failures.
references.why: Indirect rendering failures often come from count, args, binding, or bounds mismatches.
